transcription.text: Imagine a technology so powerful, that the people building it are terrified of what it might become.
Now, imagine those same creators turning against each other, locked in a bitter feud to control it.
Welcome to the defining conflict of the AI era.
This isn't just a corporate rivalry about who has the smartest chatbot.
It is a deeply personal and philosophical war.
On one side, we have Sam Altman and OpenAI, the juggernaut backed by Microsoft Azure,
moving fast, breaking barriers, and chasing the ultimate prize of artificial general intelligence,
or AGI.
On the other side, we have Dario Amade and Anthropic, a company built by OpenAI's own defectors,
determined to put safety before scale.
To understand the rivalry, we have to go back to 2016.
The tensions began in a San Francisco group house, which was home to Dario Amadei,
his sister Daniela, and Holden Karnofsky.
OpenAI co-founder Greg Brockman was a frequent visitor,
and the house became a battleground for intense debates over the future of AGI.
At one point, Brockman actually floated the idea of selling AGI to the UN Security Council's
nuclear powers.
Dario Amadei's reaction, he considered the proposal "tent amount to treason."
By 2018, Elon Musk exited OpenAI, and Sam Altman took the reins.
The internal conflicts began to boil over.
Dario even blocked Brockman from working on the critical GPT language model project,
and Daniela, who was co-leading it, threatened to step down rather than let Brockman join.
OpenAI was originally founded as a non-profit dedicated to safe research,
but it transitioned to a capped profit model, and signed a massive $1 billion partnership with
Microsoft to access the computing power needed for AGI.
For Dario, Daniela, and the safety team, this was the beginning of the end.
The breaking point arrived in 2020, when Altman accused the Amadei's of plotting against him
to the board.
The trust was completely broken, as Dario Amadei would later put it in an interview.
At the end of the day, why argue with someone when you don't have the same vision,
and you don't trust them?
Dario, Daniela, and nine other crucial employees decided to walk away.
This wasn't just a standard tech industry spin-off.
It was a cohesive exodus driven by a belief that OpenAI's commercial incentives were
overtaking its commitment to safety.
They believed the capped profit structure simply wasn't enough to hold the company's original
mission in place under extreme commercial pressure, so they founded Antropic.
Before leaving, Dario wrote a memo arguing that the ideal AI company should be 75% for the public
good and 25% for the market.
Daniela Amadei, operating as president, became the backbone of this new institution.
She implemented a rigorous mission alignment filter for hiring, even turning away top technical
talent if they didn't deeply resonate with Antropic's safety first mission.
They spent their first 18 months in absolute stealth, focusing purely on the science of AI
alignment rather than rushing a product to market.
Today, the war is fought on two distinct fronts, business architecture and technical philosophy.
OpenAI relies heavily on a technique called reinforcement learning from human feedback,
or RLHF, driven by an aggressive market strategy and deeply intertwined with Microsoft Azure.
And Thropic, however, pioneered something called constitutional AI.
Instead of relying purely on implicit human feedback, they give their AI models a literal
rulebook, a 1,200-word constitution, drawing from documents like the UN Declaration of Human Rights,
and Thropic positioned itself as the healthy alternative to OpenAI.
With Dario Amadei even going so far as to liken OpenAI to a tobacco company,
but the ripple effects of the 2020 schism didn't stop there.
Over the next few years, OpenAI faced a massive exodus of its remaining safety researchers,
key figures like Jan Lika, who co-led the super-alignment team, abruptly resigned,
stating that OpenAI was prioritizing "shiny new products" over safety guardrails,
"researcher Daniel Kokotajlo left, forfeiting roughly 85% of his family's net worth in
unvested stock, just so he could refuse to sign a restrictive non-disparagement agreement,
and speak freely about his fears that OpenAI wasn't ready to handle AGI."
Lia pulled Ashenbrenner was fired after raising internal security concerns,
and writing a memo warning that OpenAI's practices were "agregiously insufficient."
"We are now witnessing a proxy war for the future of human intelligence.
The stakes are monumental."
OpenAI recently shut down its highly anticipated Sora video app,
which was burning a million dollars a day, partly to redirect its massive computing power
to fight off and Thropic's growing dominance in the enterprise market.
We'll see who wins in the market, and we'll see who wins in the court of public opinion.
Amode recently said, "This isn't just a story about two tech companies, it's a story about human nature,
broken trust, and the terrifying responsibility of building the most consequential technology
in history. Five major companies are currently deciding the fate of our economy,
geopolitics, and society, and two of the leaders are at war, which vision of the future will win,
